coredata.dc:title: Robust state of charge estimation for lithium-ion batteries in variable temperatures using truncated singular value decomposition Cubature Kalman Filter algorithm
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tongrui
Huang, Ce
Zhu, Qiujin
coredata.dc:description: Advanced battery management systems play a significant role in the safe and efficient use of battery energy storage systems. While the accurate and reliable estimation of state of charge (SOC) is essential for improving the utilization efficiency of batteries and extending their service life. However, the working environment of battery energy storage systems is highly complex, and temperature changes can often affect the accuracy of modeling and estimation. The traditional methods of SOC estimation often ignore the changes in temperature of the whole system, thus resulting in the cumulative errors in SOC estimation. To address this problem, an improved method is proposed in this paper to estimate the SOC of lithium-ion batteries. It requires the use of Cubature Kalman Filter (CKF) algorithm that is based on Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (TSVD). Additionally, the Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares (FFRLS) method is used to determine the parameters of the Thevenin equivalent model for lithium batteries. This algorithm is then validated through simulation analysis and comparison with the experimental data obtained under cyclic operating conditions at a temperature of 5 ℃, 25 ℃, and 40 ℃, respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed TSVD-CKF algorithm is accurate in reflecting the impact of ambient temperature on the model parameters and is applicable to SOC estimation in a wide range of temperatures, showing a strong robustness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage systems
SOC
Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares
TSVD-CKF

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment and parameter identification of equivalent circuit model for batteries
Establishment of equivalent circuit model for batteries
Recursive least squares method based on forgetting factor
Volume Kalman Filter algorithm based on truncated singular values
Lithium battery SOC estimation based on TSVD-CKF algorithm
Simulation and verification of DST operating conditions at different temperatures
Verification of DST working condition algorithm under different aging states

SOC estimation results and estimation errors at 5℃.
SOC estimation results and estimation errors at 25℃.

Error Data of SOC Estimation at Different Temperatures.

head.ce:title: Robust state of charge estimation for lithium-ion batteries in variable temperatures using truncated singular value decomposition Cubature Kalman Filter algorithm

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Houston International Institute, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116086, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Houston International Institute, Dalian Maritime University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Houston International Institute, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116086, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cullen College of Engineering, The University of Houston, TX 77204, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Houston International Institute, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian 116086, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Houston International Institute, Dalian Maritime University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advanced battery management systems play a significant role in the safe and efficient use of battery energy storage systems. While the accurate and reliable estimation of state of charge (SOC) is essential for improving the utilization efficiency of batteries and extending their service life. However, the working environment of battery energy storage systems is highly complex, and temperature changes can often affect the accuracy of modeling and estimation. The traditional methods of SOC estimation often ignore the changes in temperature of the whole system, thus resulting in the cumulative errors in SOC estimation. To address this problem, an improved method is proposed in this paper to estimate the SOC of lithium-ion batteries. It requires the use of Cubature Kalman Filter (CKF) algorithm that is based on Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (TSVD). Additionally, the Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares (FFRLS) method is used to determine the parameters of the Thevenin equivalent model for lithium batteries. This algorithm is then validated through simulation analysis and comparison with the experimental data obtained under cyclic operating conditions at a temperature of 5 ℃, 25 ℃, and 40 ℃, respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed TSVD-CKF algorithm is accurate in reflecting the impact of ambient temperature on the model parameters and is applicable to SOC estimation in a wide range of temperatures, showing a strong robustness.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) play a major role in energy supply for battery energy storage systems (BESSs). They demonstrate such advantages as high power density, high energy density, long cycle life, and low self-discharge rate, which makes them widely applicable in BESSs [1]. To ensure the safe and reliable operation of lithium-ion battery energy storage systems, the Battery Management System (BMS) is required to continuously monitor and manage their status in real time. The accurate calculation of battery state of charge (SOC) is one of the crucial tasks performed by BMS. A precise estimation of SOC is conducive to enhancing the utilization efficiency of batteries, extending their service life, and ensuring the safe and reliable operation of battery packs in the energy storage systems [2]. However, it is difficult to measure the SOC of batteries directly, which means it can only be estimated using certain algorithms [3]. Therefore, it is challenging to estimate SOC for the batteries operating at different temperatures. At present, many methods have been proposed to study the SOC of lithium-ion batteries. In general, they are classified into open-loop experimental methods, data-driven methods, model-based methods, and other fusion-based methods [4].

The mainstream open-loop methods include the ampere hour integration method [5] and the open circuit voltage method [6]. The Anshi integration method is very simple. However, as the calculation process continues, the error becomes increasingly significant, which renders SOC estimation inaccurate [7]. Despite the high accuracy of SOC estimation by the open circuit voltage method, it still requires offline measurement for the open circuit voltage of the battery, thus making it unsuitable for battery management systems [8].

A data-driven approach requires the use of data in large amounts. By learning from the existing experimental data, a "black box" model is constructed to approximate the experimental data infinitely. Then, the input of this model is used to estimate the SOC of the battery. For instance, Artificial Neural Network (ANN) [9,10] and Radial Basis Function Neural Network (RBFNN) [11] are used to train various data such as battery current, voltage, internal resistance, etc. Thus, their relationship with the SOC of the battery is obtained to complete SOC estimation for the battery. By integrating the BP neural network algorithm with the genetic algorithm, Liu et al. [12] estimated the SOC of the battery in real time and improved the accuracy of SOC estimation. However, an ideal condition is required by all the above algorithms for a satisfactory performance in SOC estimation. Besides, the amount and reliability of data generated by batteries clearly fall short of expectations, which limits the application of the above algorithms in practice.

Currently, model-based methods are widely used for state of charge (SOC) estimation. It starts by establishing an electrochemical model (EM) of the battery [13,14] or an equivalent circuit model (ECM) [15]. Also, EM is used to construct a model by exploring the mechanism of chemical reaction occurring in the batteries. The ECM is built using the basic circuit components to simulate the characteristics of the battery in resistance and capacitance. Compared with EM, ECM requires fewer model parameters and a simpler calculation process, thus making it widely applicable for the modeling lithium-ion batteries used in vehicles [16]. Westerhoff et al. [17] estimated SOC by establishing the relationship between various components and SOC in the equivalent circuit model of lithium-ion batteries. Liu Jialin et al. [18] performed filtering via the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) to estimate the SOC of lithium-ion batteries. Yue Wei et al. [19] developed a fractional-order H ∞ observer and applied it to SOC estimation for lithium-ion battery packs. They established a fractional-order equivalent circuit model based on fractional capacitance and proposed a method of SOC estimation based on the fractional-order H ∞ observer. Liu Xi et al. [20] used Kalman Filter (KF) and EKF in combination to estimate the SOC and model parameters of the battery. However, the above literature overlooks the impact of temperature on SOC estimation for lithium-ion batteries, which reduces the accuracy of SOC estimation.

Among various model-based methods, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) [21] and Particle Filter (PF) are commonly used to estimate SOC of the battery [22]. However, EKF is a process of linearization for addressing the nonlinear problems with significant truncation errors that affect the accuracy of estimation. In PF, a particle set is used to represent probability, express its distribution based on the Monte Carlo method from posterior probability density, obtain a probability distribution function, and estimate the posterior probability density with sample point estimation. However, it is prone to particle degradation and excessive calculation [23]. Cubature Kalman Filter (CKF) follows the third-order spherical radial rules, which are simple and reliable to implement, showing a high accuracy in filtering, and compensates for the respective shortcomings of EKF and PF [24]. However, the state covariance matrix is gradually compromised on its non-negative definiteness after repeated filtering of CKF, which is due to the accumulation of round-off errors. Besides, the accuracy of filtering declines or diverges. To address this problem, Liu et al. [25] combined CKF with Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) for SOC estimation of the battery and validated this method under various working conditions. However, the current transformation of CKF focuses on adaptability, ignoring the optimization of filter robustness. Due to the variation in ambient temperature, there is uncertainty in model parameters of the battery. Thus, the accuracy of estimation by the filter is significantly affected and filter divergence even occurs as a result. Thus, it is essential to ensure that the filter is robust in the presence of uncertain model parameters.

In light of this, a Cubature Kalman Filter (CKF) algorithm is proposed in this paper on the basis of Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (TSVD) to achieve an accurate estimation of State of Charge (SOC) for the battery. The innovation of this article is reflected in the following aspects:
1)
1). The Forgetting Factor Recursive Least Squares (FFRLS) method is used to identify the model parameters online, which makes it possible to track time-varying parameters in the model rapidly, estimate the parameters accurately, and achieve an excellent performance in parameter identification under various SOC conditions.
2)
2). The battery SOC estimation method based on TSVD-CKF is applied to ensure the high accuracy of estimation by the filter when temperature changes cause uncertainty in model parameters and to effectively enhance robustness of the filter.
3)
3). The above algorithm is experimentally verified at different temperatures and under different operating conditions. The results demonstrate that the algorithm based on TSVD-CKF is accurate in monitoring SOC and is highly robust.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment and parameter identification of equivalent circuit model for batteries
Establishment of equivalent circuit model for batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Since the state of charge (SOC) of a battery cannot be directly measured, an accurate battery model must be established to estimate it. The battery model relies on mathematical relationships to characterize the relationship between the influencing factors of the battery and its external characteristics. Among many battery models, the Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) is widely used in many battery state estimation methods due to its excellent adaptability to the environmental conditions and low complexity. The commonly used ECM models include Rint, PNGV, Thevenin, GNL, and second-order RC equivalent circuits [26]. Since the research object of this paper is a lithium iron phosphate battery, literature [27] compares different types of ECMs and finds out that the Thevenin model gives careful consideration to model accuracy and computation, and its comprehensive performance is optimal [28]. Therefore, this paper uses the Thevenin model to establish the ECM. The structure of the Thevenin model is shown in Fig. 1.

These factors include the Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV), ohmic internal resistance, polarization effect resistance, and the polarization effect capacitance of the battery, represented by
U
O C

, respectively. The terminal voltage and charge/discharge current of the battery are also represented by
U ,
I

, respectively.

According to Thevenin's theorem, the battery terminal voltage can be expressed U as:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recursive least squares method based on forgetting factor
ce:section.ce:para: The non-linearity and time-varying nature of lithium-ion batteries necessitate the identification of model parameters over time during usage. As a result, an online identification method is required to determine the model parameters. However, with each iteration added to the ordinary least squares, the entire algorithm must be re-executed, leading to the extended computational time of the least squares matrix. Consequently, online identification calculation is prolonged. To address this problem, this paper proposes the use of the recursive least squares method to reduce computational time and the difficulty in online identification. The principle of calculation is as follows.

Nevertheless, the recursive least squares method shows a tendency to accumulate and increase original data as the number of steps in calculation increases. This accumulation makes it challenging for new input data to correct the previous estimated values, thereby affecting the subsequent battery estimation performance. To circumvent this issue, this paper introduces a forgetting factor and selects the FFRLS method.
(11)
J =
∑
k = 1
N
λ
N − k
[
y
k
−
Φ
k
T
θ ˆ
k − 1
]
2

The FFRLS method is obtained for the performance indicator Eq. (11), which is based on the recursive least squares method.
(12)
θ ˆ
k
=
θ ˆ
k − 1
+
K
k

Φ

θ ˆ

]
(13)
K

=
P
k − 1
Φ

λ +
Φ

T
P

Φ
k
(14)
P
k
=
1 λ
(
P
k − 1
−
K
k
Φ
k
T
P
k − 1
)

In the above equation,
θ ˆ
k − 1
represents the estimated parameter value at the previous time,
Φ
k
T
θ ˆ
k − 1
represents the observed value at the current time, and
y
k
represents the actual observed value at the current time. The difference between the observed value at the current time and the observed value at the current time is defined as the expected error.
K
k
represents the gain term. The corrected value of the predicted value at the current time is obtained by multiplying the expected error with the gain term. The estimated value at the current time is then calculated as the sum of the corrected value of the predicted value at the current time and the estimated value at the previous time.

The range of values for the forgetting factor is
0.95 < λ < 1
. Denoted as λ , it generally has an impact on the tracking ability of the algorithm. Specifically, a smaller value of A can lead to a stronger tracking ability, but it may also cause fluctuations in the algorithm and reduce the speed of convergence. As λ approaches 1, the convergence of the algorithm is accelerated, but the tracking ability is weakened. When
λ = 1
, the FFRLS algorithm is reduced to the ordinary recursive least squares method. Therefore, the appropriate value of A depends on the desired filtering performance of the FFRLS method. The recursive least squares method for parameter identification based on the forgetting factor is illustrated in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Volume Kalman Filter algorithm based on truncated singular values

ce:section.ce:para:
The SVD-CKF algorithm replaces the Cholesky decomposition used in the standard CKF algorithm with singular value decomposition when the error covariance matrix is decomposed and iterated. This not only avoids the problem of non-positive definite phenomena that may arise in the covariance matrix of the system state equation due to Cholesky decomposition, but also improves the robustness of the CKF algorithm. A nonlinear system with R is considered as a set of n-dimensional real vectors. The state equation and measurement equation of the system are expressed as follows:
(15)

x

= f

,

)

v
k
where A represents the state vector, B represents the measurement vector, C represents the nonlinear function, D represents the measurement matrix, and E and F represent the process noise and measurement noise of the system, respectively.
In the case of the nonlinear system (15), the SVD-CKF algorithm is computed according to the following process:

The research on the issues caused by singular value decomposition in SVD-CKF is presented below, with a linear equation system as an example:
(35)
y = C ⋅ x

Perform singular value decomposition on the coefficient matrix C in Eq. (35)
(36)
C = U Λ
V
T

In Eq. (38),
σ
1
,

, ⋯ ,
σ
p
is the p non zero singular values of matrix C, and
σ
1

. Substituting Eqs. (34), (35), and (36) into (33) yields the solution of x as follows:
(39)
x
p
=
∑
i = 1
p
σ
i
− 1
v
i
u
i
T
⋅ y

The expression of the solution in Eq. (39) reveals that singular values appear in their reciprocal form. Singular values have been identified as the "essential information" contained within data [29]. However, as the reciprocal of singular values becomes smaller, their small changes can have a significant impact on the stability of the solution. During the filtering process, the change in model parameters inevitably interferes with the stability of the filtering estimation value. Smaller singular values can result in larger errors. Therefore, retaining only larger singular values in the solution is equivalent to discarding minimal data information while enhancing the ability of the filter to resist noise data interference to a certain extent. This often leads to relatively stable estimation results.

Thus, this article further investigates the properties of singular values. The distribution of singular values in the matrix is arranged in descending order. In general, the sum of the first 10% of singular values accounts for 90% of all singular values [30]. Most of the information is contained within the first 10% of singular values during the process of singular value decomposition.

Therefore, this article proposes the elimination of smaller singular values and the retention of larger singular values in Eqs. (2–25). By discarding a smaller amount of information, the stability of the solution can be improved. With only the first singular value assumed to be retained, the solution obtained by truncating the singular value is expressed as follows:
(40)
x
t
=
∑
i = 1
t
σ
i
− 1
v
i
u
i
T
⋅ y
Obviously, the key to determining the calculation accuracy of this method is to determine the appropriate truncation threshold t .

The selection of the truncation threshold t is crucial for the process of TSVD. If the value of t is too large, some small non-zero singular values may not be discarded, leading to unstable solutions and poor robustness of the filter. Conversely, if the value of 't' is too small, some information may be lost, resulting in a low accuracy of the solution. This situation is analogous to the principle of selecting Tikhonov regularization parameters. Therefore, this paper utilizes the L-curve method [31] to determine the truncation threshold of singular value decomposition in the process of determining regularization parameters. The process of using the L-curve method to determine regularization parameters is detailed first. For a regularization solution:
(41)
x ˆ
γ
=
(
A
T
A + α
L
T
L )
− 1
A
T
y

The regularization parameter is denoted as α . The fitting part of the model is represented by
η
γ
=
‖
A
x
γ
− y
‖
, while the smoothing part is represented by
δ
γ
=
‖
L
x ˆ
γ
‖
. For the varying values of α , the corresponding values of
( log
δ
γ
,
log
η
γ
)
are determined and tabulated on a coordinate plane to obtain an L-shaped curve. The horizontal and vertical coordinates correspond to the fitted and smoothed parts of the model, respectively. The regularization parameter is selected by identifying the α value that corresponds to the point with the largest curvature on the curve or the point closest to the coordinate origin, thus achieving a reasonable allocation of smoothing and fitting. In this article, the L-curve method is used to determine the truncation threshold of singular value decomposition. Specifically, Eq. (40) is utilized to obtain the L-curve.
(42)
δ
q
2
=
‖
x
q
‖
2
=
∑
i = 1
q

T
y

)
2
(43)

A

∑
i = 1

Subsequently, the corresponding values of
δ
q
2
and
η
q
2
are computed for different q values, and the corresponding
( log
δ
q
,
log
η
q
)
values are recorded on the coordinate plane for each q value. As the truncation threshold value is a discrete positive integer, a dashed L-shaped line, comprised of q discrete points, is plotted on the coordinate plane. The optimal truncation threshold value is selected by identifying the q value that corresponds to the point closest to the coordinate origin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lithium battery SOC estimation based on TSVD-CKF algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, the TVSD-CKF algorithm is proposed to address the issue that the accuracy in estimating state of charge (SOC) for the battery is reduced by model parameter uncertainty at different temperatures. The algorithm, which is based on the CKF framework, introduces the TSVD transform during time updates to improve the robustness to numerical calculations and reduce computational complexity [32]. In the TVSD-CKF filtering process, the singular value decomposition (SVD) with strong numerical stability is performed to replace the Cholesky decomposition of traditional CKF. This section presents the theoretical derivation process of this algorithm.

SVD decomposition is a method that can be applied to any matrix. It represents a complex matrix as the product of several relatively simple matrices, such as eigenvalues and eigenvectors, thereby simplifying operations while maintaining the characteristics of the original matrix [33].

In CKF, the covariance matrix P is calculated multiple times and is no longer non-negative definite. However, it remains an n-order symmetric matrix in form. It is assumed that its rank is r, and
n > r > 0
. Among all the singular values obtained by SVD of the covariance matrix P, a smaller singular value reflects the severity of complex collinearity. Removing the smaller singular value during the calculation can reduce the complex collinearity of the coefficient matrix and obtain the solutions that are more stable and accurate.

The singular value decomposition of matrix 'P' is expressed as follows:
(44)
P = U
[
Z
0
0
0
]
V
T

Among them
U =
[
u
1
,
u
2
, ⋯ ,
u
n
]
,
V =
[
v
1
,
v
2
, ⋯ ,
v
n
]
,
Z = d i a g (
σ
1
2
,
σ
2
2
, ⋯ ,
σ
r
2
)
,
σ
1
2
≥
σ
2
2
≥ ⋯ ≥
σ
r
2
,where
σ
1
2
,
σ
2
2
, ⋯ ,
σ
r
2
is the non-zero singular value of matrix P, and contented with
σ
1
2
≥
σ
2
2
≥ ⋯ ≥
σ
r
2
.

The removal of smaller singular values near zero in Z in Eq. (7) can significantly enhance the robustness of SOC estimation. In Eq. (7), only the first d (where
d < r
) singular values are retained, with the d -th value being the truncated value. Consequently, Eq. (7) is transformed into:
(45)
P = U
[
S
d
0
0
0
]
U
T
TSVD-CKF decomposition
If the Cholesky factorization factor of the covariance matrix P is S , the basic steps of TSVD-CKF are as follows:

(1) Calculate the cubature sampling points and corresponding weights:
(46)
ξ
i
=
m 2
[ 1 ]
i
(47)
ω
i
=
1 m
where m represents the number of sampling points,
[ 1 ]
i
represents the i-th point in the point set [1], and [1] represents the complete symmetric point set.

(2) Perform time update, and calculate cabature points:
(48)
P
k − 1
=
U
k − 1
S
k − 1

(49)

(50)

)

Calculate the prediction state and prediction variance matrix:
(51)

(3) Perform measurement update and factorization:
(53)

Calculate the cabature points:
(54)

(55)

Calculate measurement predictions, innovation variance, and covariance estimates:
(56)

Calculate the gain matrix, and update the state and covariance:
(59)

(61)

K

P
k / k − 1
z z
K
k
T

The application of TVSD decomposition shows several advantages. Firstly, it can effectively decompose negative definite matrices, thereby facilitating the continuation of the algorithm and improving operational accuracy. Secondly, it can avoid square root operations, thus ensuring numerical robustness during iteration. From a computational perspective, the TSVD decomposition of 'n'-th order matrices requires slightly less computation than Cholesky decomposition. Thus, TSVD decomposition exhibits higher computational efficiency.

The experimental object is the Cylindrical Lithium-ion Cell, model LR18650EH, and the specific parameters of the battery are shown in Table 1. In Fig. 3, the red dotted line on the left is an incubator, the red dotted line on the right is the Arbin instrument ‘LBT-5V30A’ selected as the battery charge test equipment, and the green dotted line on the right represents the battery samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation and verification of DST operating conditions at different temperatures
ce:section.ce:para:
In this paper, the constant current discharge method is adopted to fit the 9th order data of open circuit voltage
U
O C
and SOC, through which the OCV-SOC curve is drawn. As the external temperature fluctuates, the battery shows changes in the trend of OCV-SOC. Fig. 4 shows the curve fitting results obtained at the three different temperatures. The OCV-SOC curve at 25℃ and that at 40 ℃ clearly overlap. Also, there is a significant difference observed between both curves and the curve at 5 ℃. This is because a rise in ohmic internal resistance and polarization internal resistance increases the terminal voltage and reduces open circuit voltage at low temperatures.

The accuracy of the battery model was verified using the FFRLS parameter identification method with the DST operating current as excitation. Fig. 5 displays the terminal voltage error curves at different temperatures, and Table 2 presents the main indicators of measurement. From the figure, it can be seen that the terminal voltage average mean error (AVME) of all models is 2.42 mV, and the maximum mean absolute error (MAE) is 0.82 mV. The model exhibits high accuracy and satisfies the requirements on state of charge (SOC) estimation for the battery.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the algorithm proposed in this article, experiments were conducted to estimate the state of charge (SOC) for the battery at three different temperatures: 5 ℃, 25 ℃, and 40 ℃. The SOC estimation results and the corresponding errors at each temperature are presented in
Figs. 6, 7, and 8, respectively.

Table 3 presents the primary data indicators for battery SOC estimation at different temperatures, with the maximum error found to be only 3.62%. This result indicates that the error in estimating SOC at different temperatures through the TSVD-CKF algorithm is within the expected range, and its excellent performance is demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of DST working condition algorithm under different aging states
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the influence of the aging characteristics of lithium-ion batteries on the SOC algorithm proposed in this paper, experiments and simulation validation were conducted under the conditions of DST operation. The terminal voltage error curves of lithium-ion batteries after 1, 300, and 637 cycles at 25 ℃ are depicted in the figure below. Fig. 9(d) and 9 (e) display the terminal voltage error curves of lithium-ion batteries after 1 cycle at 5 ℃ and 637 cycles at 40 ℃, respectively.

Fig. 10 displays the primary measurement indicators of all models in the form of a histogram. The maximum absolute error of the absolute value of maximum error (AVME) in all models is 11.4 mV, the root mean square error (RMSE) is 0.49 mV, and the mean absolute error (MAE) is 0.29 mV. Considering that the range of operating voltage in the batteries used in this experiment is between 2.0 V and 3.65 V, it can be inferred that the accuracy of the proposed model, accounting for the impact of battery aging factors, can still meet the requirements on battery state estimation.
To validate the algorithm proposed in this paper, comparative experiments were carried out by exciting SVD-CKF, CKF, and EKF at the same DST operating current.

Table 4 lists the errors of several algorithms in estimating the state of charge (SOC) for batteries. Due to the significant error in initial convergence of the algorithm, only the data after the algorithm tends to stabilize is shown here. Apparently, the maximum error of the CKF algorithm reaches 4.27%, while the maximum error of the TSVD-CKF-based algorithm is merely 2.01%. The accuracy achieved by the traditional extended Kalman filter algorithm is approximately 2.21%. Notably, the SVD-CKF algorithm proposed in this paper is applicable to SOC estimation for lithium-ion batteries at different ambient temperatures. Also, the accuracy of estimation is maintained at high levels under different aging conditions of the lithium-ion batteries.

Given different ambient temperatures and aging conditions, the parameter identification and SOC estimation methods suitable for lithium-ion battery models perform poorly in robustness, which results in the cumulative errors in SOC estimation. To address these issues, an algorithm based on TSVD-CKF is proposed in this paper. It adopts the forgetting factor-based recursive least squares method to identify the model parameters of the Thevenin equivalent model for lithium-ion batteries. Both simulation analysis and comparative experiment are performed to validate the algorithm under cyclic operating conditions. According to the simulation results, the AVME obtained by SOC estimation is 2.42 mV at maximum, and the MAE is 0.82 mV at maximum, indicating a relative consistency in error. The accuracy of estimation by the algorithm is verified with the DST operating current as excitation at an ambient temperature of 5 ℃, 25 ℃, and 40 ℃, respectively. The estimated AVME of SOC is 2.01% at maximum. To sum up, the TSVD-CKF algorithm proposed in this paper is accurate in reflecting the impact of ambient temperature on model parameters. It is applicable to SOC estimation in a wide range of temperatures, showing a strong robustness.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State of the art of lithium-ion battery SOC estimation for electrical vehicles
Energies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Zhang, B. Xia, B. Li, L. Cao, Y. Lai, W. Zhang, H. Wang, W.Wang, State of the art of lithium-ion battery SOC estimation for electrical vehicles, Energies. 11. 7 (2018) 1820. https://doi.org/10.3390/en11071820
